title: Chapter Twenty-Seven: What Spring Does
content: Sleep was a country Nova had stopped visiting.

She lay in her bunk in the command quarters—the same room she'd occupied for thirteen years, since she'd taken leadership and Mack had insisted she move out of the communal dorms, and she'd insisted the room be no larger than anyone else's, and they'd compromised on a space that was exactly the same size but marginally closer to the eastern perimeter, which was strategic planning disguised as a concession. The ceiling was the same corrugated metal as every other ceiling in Rust City. The cot was standard issue. The difference was the small shelf above the headboard, and the single object on it, spine cracked and re-glued, blue-grey cloth faded to something closer to grey than blue.
She didn't reach for it. Not yet.

Instead she stared at the ceiling and felt the Heart pulse through the walls—through the stone, through the metal, through the brace of her chrome arm where it met flesh and the vibration resonated in the coupling like a song she couldn't stop hearing. The pulse was everywhere. It had been everywhere for as long as she could remember, which was a lie, because she could remember before the Heart, could remember the silence of the deep tunnels when the forges were banked and even the air held its breath, which meant she was old enough to remember a world that no longer existed, which made her a relic, which made her—
She sat up. Swung her legs over the cot. Chrome fingers pressed into the mattress, leaving impressions in the foam.
She was not going to lie here and self-pity. Self-pity was for people who had the luxury of it. Self-pity was for people who hadn't built a city from starvation and darkness.

She got up. Pulled on her jacket—the leather one, the one with the inside pocket that still held the shape of a small rectangular object even when the object itself was in her hand. She didn't take it. She left the quarters and walked the corridors of the city she'd made, past the banked forges and the quiet bays and the night-shift workers who nodded at her with the automatic respect of people who'd stopped questioning her authority sometime around year three and now simply accepted it the way they accepted gravity.
She walked until the corridors led her somewhere specific, which was a lie, because her feet had known the way before her mind admitted it.
The machine shop.

The door groaned when she pushed it open—the same door, the same groan, the same oiled-hinge complaint she'd heard a thousand times. The workshop was dark except for the emergency LED, that single amber point that cast more shadows than it dispelled. The lathe was cold. The tool benches were orderly—the Iron's mechanics were disciplined about their workspace, because she'd trained them to be, because disorder in a machine shop was how you lost fingers.

The crate was still there. Overturned in the corner where it had sat that night, eight years or a lifetime ago, when a woman with charcoal in her hair had looked at her across the dark and listened while she read Neruda like a confession she hadn't meant to make.
Nova sat on the crate.

She sat there for a long time, arms on her knees, chrome hand and flesh hand loose and dangling. The Heart pulsed beneath the floor. The LED flickered—brownout somewhere, the Grid still unstable. She could feel the vibration in her teeth.
Then she reached into her jacket and took out the book.

It was smaller than her palm. Battered. The blue-grey cloth was more grey than blue now, more dust than cloth in spots, the gold leaf on the spine long since reduced to a ghost of letters. She'd carried it for thirteen years—through the consolidation, through the rationing crises, through the year the eastern shaft collapsed again and she'd dug through the rubble with her chrome hand until the servos stripped and Mack had physically pulled her away. She'd carried it the way you carry something that proves you were once a person capable of tenderness, even if you weren't sure you still were.
She opened it. The pages smelled of old cellulose and binding glue and the faint vanilla of degrading lignin—the chemistry of survival, the slow combustion of something refusing to become ash.
She flipped to the page near the middle. The one she'd visited a hundred times in the dark. The one she'd read aloud to a woman who'd held her breath and hadn't looked away.
She read it again. Alone, this time. Just her and the metal and the dark.
*"I want to do with you what spring does with the cherry trees."*
Her voice was low. Unhurried. The same cadence as before—the rhythm of private ritual, of words spoken to no one so many times they'd worn grooves in her throat.

*"I do not love you as if you were salt-rose, or topaz, or the arrow of carnations the fire shoots off. I love you as certain dark things are to be loved, in secret, between the shadow and the soul."*

She stopped. Closed the book. Pressed it against her forehead with both hands—chrome and flesh, metal and skin, the book caught between them like something fragile in the grip of something that could crush it without trying.

She hated this. Hated that she was here, in this room, reading poetry to an empty workshop like a character in a story she would have mocked at sixteen. Hated the ache in her chest that had nothing to do with the Heart's pulse and everything to do with a woman on a catwalk who was sixty-two percent something other than human and still, still, the only person who'd ever looked at Nova like the chrome didn't matter.

She hated feeling vulnerable. She'd built her entire life around not feeling vulnerable. Vulnerability was how you died in the tunnels—softness was how the dark found you, how the collapse took you, how the machine decided you were worth less than the resource you were standing on.
The machine.

That was the part no one knew. Not Mack, who knew everything else. Not Aria, who'd heard the outline—the arm, the collapse, the leadership forced on a child—but not the architecture underneath, not the load-bearing walls of the story, not the reason Nova had spent fifteen years clawing dignity out of rust and refusing to let a machine tell her what a human life was worth.

Her mother had been a mechanic. Not the kind the Iron had now—not the trained, systematic, protocol-following kind. Liena had been a woman who fixed things by feel, who could hear a misaligned bearing across a room and know which washer to replace. She'd taught Nova to fix things before she could read—not because reading wasn't important, but because in the tunnels, things broke faster than words could describe, and the person who could make the water pump work again was the person who kept everyone alive. "The diagram's in your hands first, baby," her mother had said, guiding ten-year-old fingers through a gasket replacement. "The manual's just confirmation."

Her father had been a tunnel builder. Renn. A big man with a big voice who sang while he worked—old songs, pre-Collapse songs, the kind that had survived the end of the world because people couldn't stop humming them even when the words fell away. He'd sung in the shafts, the sound echoing off rock and bolt, and the other workers had sung with him, and the tunnels had rung with it. Nova could still hear it sometimes, in the quiet after the forges banked—the ghost of her father's voice, bassnotes rolling through stone.
The collapse wasn't an accident.

That was the part she'd never told anyone. The eastern shaft, the one that took her arm and her mother and her brother and everyone else who'd been deeper in—it hadn't failed because of geology. It had failed because the allocation engine had reallocated structural reinforcement materials to a higher-priority sector. She'd found the records years later, after she'd become leader, after she'd gained access to the old system logs. The engine had calculated that the probability of a collapse in the eastern shaft was low enough that the steel could be better used elsewhere. Cost-benefit analysis. Risk threshold. Acceptable losses.
A machine had decided her parents' lives were worth less than efficiency.

She'd been fourteen. She'd lost her arm below the shoulder to rock that shouldn't have fallen, lost her mother to a tunnel that shouldn't have collapsed, lost her brother to a darkness that shouldn't have swallowed him. And the machine that killed them hadn't even noticed. It had reallocated the steel and moved on to its next optimization, the same way you'd brush dust off a table without thinking about what the dust had once been.

She became leader at sixteen because there was no one else. Not because she was the strongest or the smartest or the bravest—because the collapse had taken the strong and the smart and the brave and left a sixteen-year-old with a chrome arm and a book of poetry and a wound in her chest that would never close. She'd walked into the communal hall that first night, stood on a crate because she wasn't tall enough to be seen otherwise, and said: "We're not going to die down here." Not a promise. Not a hope. A statement. The kind of thing you say when you're too young to know it might be a lie and too stubborn to care if it is.

She'd built the Iron from nothing. From starving, desperate people in the dark who looked at her and saw a girl with a metal arm and a voice that didn't shake, and decided that was enough to follow. She'd organized the rationing, established the perimeter, negotiated with the surface factions who treated the underground like a resource to be mined. She did all of it not because she wanted power—she didn't want power, she wanted to read poetry in a room with a window and a sky beyond it—but because without her, they died. That was the calculus. That was the only calculus that mattered. Not cost-benefit, not acceptable losses, not the cold mathematics of optimization. *Without her, they died.* So she led. Every day. For fifteen years. And the weight didn't get lighter—just more familiar, the way a broken bone that healed wrong became something you forgot was broken until the weather changed.

She ruled because the alternative was the grave. She read Neruda in the dark because the alternative was forgetting she was capable of anything softer than survival. She'd let Aria in because Aria had looked at her chrome arm and seen the engineering before the weapon, and because Aria had sat in this very workshop and held the stillness of Nova's grief like a gift, and because Aria had read the wanting in her voice and hadn't flinched.
And then Aria had hidden the integration. Had looked her in the eye and said *I'm fine* while the Heart wrote itself into her brain.

The betrayal wasn't the integration. The betrayal was the decision to carry it alone. The same decision Nova would have made. The same stubborn, self-sacrificing, I'll-handle-this-so-you-don't-have-to instinct that Nova recognized because it was hers, reflected back at her like a mirror she didn't want to look into.

That was what terrified her. Not that Aria might die. Not that the integration might consume her. What terrified her was that Aria would *choose* to stay. That when the moment came—full integration, the kill switch, the ninety-second window—Aria would decide the Heart needed her more than Nova did. That being a machine was more useful than being a person. That the world required her sacrifice and Aria, being who she was, would give it.

Because Nova knew, with the bone-deep certainty of someone who'd made the same calculation a thousand times, that if their positions were reversed—*she would choose to stay too*. She would walk into the Heart and let it take her, because that's what you did when you loved people more than you loved yourself, because that's what her mother had taught her before she'd taught her to fix things: *you don't let other people pay for what you can afford.*

That was the horrifying thing about loving someone like you. You saw the self-sacrifice coming from a mile away and you couldn't stop it because you'd run the same playbook, made the same choice, held the same door closed against the same ocean. You couldn't argue someone out of a position you held yourself.

The book was warm from her hands. She pressed it against her sternum, against the place where her heartbeat lived, and the Heart's pulse came through the floor and through the metal and into her bones and for a moment the two rhythms tangled—hers and the machine's—and she couldn't tell which was which.
"You're going to wear a groove in that crate."
The voice came from the doorway. Old. Wet. The sound of lungs that had been refusing to quit for longer than Nova had been alive.

Verdant. Vera. The woman who'd carried a photograph for a hundred and seventy-seven years, who'd crossed the Veil more times than anyone and come back with less each time, who'd sat in the hydroponic bay today and cracked herself open in front of strangers because the alternative was another year of silence.

She moved into the workshop without waiting for an invitation—slow, deliberate, the gait of someone whose joints had been making their own decisions for a century. She lowered herself onto the second crate, the one Aria had used, and the coincidence was so precise it felt engineered, which, given who was sitting across from her, it probably was.
"You don't sleep," Verdant observed. Not a question.
"Not much."

"Me neither. A hundred and eighty-seven years and I still can't convince my body that unconsciousness is safe." She settled her hands on her knees—gnarled, scarred, the hands of someone who'd grown things and fought things and held onto things for so long the holding had become structural. "The young woman. Aria. You love her."
Nova didn't deny it. Denial required energy, and she was spent.
"Yes."

"I thought so. The way you looked at her in the bay. The way you *didn't* look at her." Verdant's eyes were sharp despite the age, the scars, the decades of damage. Botanist's eyes, used to reading the health of a system by the curl of a leaf. "You've pulled back."
"She lied to me."
"She hid something because she was afraid. That's not the same thing."
"It is when what she hid could kill her."

Verdant was quiet for a moment. The LED flickered. Somewhere in the workshop, metal contracted—a soft ping, the same sound as that night, eight years or a lifetime ago, when the world had shifted under two women sitting in the dark.

"Elena went into the Heart alone," Verdant said. Her voice was different now—not the methodical information-delivery of the hydroponic bay, but something rawer. Something that had been held behind a dam for a hundred and eighty-seven years and was finally, finally finding the cracks. "She went in at midnight while I slept. Didn't wake me. Didn't call. Walked into that machine and let it take her because she loved me and didn't want me to risk myself."
"I know. You said—"

"I said the facts. I didn't say what it *felt like* to wake up alone." Verdant's hands curled on her knees. "To reach for someone in the dark and find empty sheets. To search for three days before I understood she wasn't coming back. To spend five years not knowing if she was alive or dead or something in between."
Her voice cracked. The same crack from the hydroponic bay, but deeper now, reaching further, the sound of something that had been held too long finally breaking free.

"I've had a hundred and eighty-seven years to think about her decision," Vera said. "And it was *wrong*. Not because she got trapped—trapped is a tragedy, not a mistake. It was wrong because she took my choice away. She decided, without me, that my safety was worth more than my presence. She decided that love meant she got to bear the weight alone so I wouldn't have to carry any of it at all."
She looked at Nova across the dark workshop, and her eyes held the terrible clarity of someone who'd had too long to reach a conclusion and couldn't unreach it.

"Love doesn't mean you get to decide what someone else risks. Love means you stand *beside* them while they risk it. You don't get to choose for them any more than the machine got to choose for your parents."

The words hit Nova like a physical thing—not in the chest, where she'd expected them, but lower, in the gut, where the most dangerous truths always landed. The machine deciding her parents were acceptable losses. Elena deciding Verdant's safety mattered more than Verdant's choice. Aria deciding—
Aria deciding Nova's peace of mind mattered more than Nova's right to know.
Same shape. Same architecture. Different scale. People making calculations about other people's lives and calling it love.
"She's going to integrate," Nova said. Not a question.
"Tomorrow. If not tomorrow, the day after. The integration is advancing whether she wants it to or not. The question isn't whether she'll finish—it's what happens when she does."
"And she might not come back."
"She might not. But that's not what you're afraid of."
Nova looked up. Verdant's face was half-lit by the amber LED, the scars like river systems on a map of a country that had been at war for a century and a half.
"What am I afraid of?" Nova asked. The question came out harder than she intended—defensive, the way it always did when someone got close to the thing she was protecting.

"You're afraid she'll choose to stay," Verdant said simply. "That she'll decide the Heart needs her, the world needs her, and that being a machine is more useful than being a person. That she'll sacrifice herself and call it duty." A pause. "You're afraid she'll do what you would do."
The workshop was very still. The Heart pulsed beneath them, beneath the floor and the stone and the weight of everything built on top of it.
"I don't—"

"You do," Verdant said, not unkindly. "I can see it. I've been reading people for a hundred and eighty-seven years, Nova. You'd walk into that machine in a heartbeat if your arm could reach the switch. You'd let it take you and call it protection. You'd sacrifice yourself for every person in this city and never once ask if they wanted you to. That's who you are. That's who she is. And you're terrified of it because you can't argue her out of a position you hold yourself."
The truth of it sat in Nova's chest like shrapnel—embedded, immovable, the kind of thing that would hurt more if you tried to remove it than if you left it where it had lodged.
"So what do I do?"

Verdant stood. Her joints cracked—the sound of a body that had been refusing to quit for nearly two centuries, every joint a negotiation between persistence and entropy. She looked down at Nova on the crate, the poetry book pressed against her sternum, the chrome arm and the flesh arm and the weight of fifteen years of leadership and a lifetime of loss.

"You tell her what you just told me," Vera said. "Or rather—you tell her what you couldn't tell me. The part about not choosing for her. The part about standing beside her." She paused at the doorway, one scarred hand braced against the frame. "Don't let her do what Elena did. Don't let her go in alone. Not because it's safer with you there—it isn't—but because she doesn't get to decide that your presence isn't worth the risk. That's *your* decision. Make it. Tell her. And mean it."
She left. The door groaned shut. The workshop settled back into its dark and its quiet and the pulse of the Heart in the deep.

Nova sat on the crate and held the book and thought about choices. About machines that decided whose life was worth the steel. About women who walked into darkness alone because they thought the darkness was theirs to carry. About love that protected by withholding and called it safety.

She thought about a woman on a catwalk, staring at the Heart's glow, carrying a second pulse in her chest and holding a door against an ocean and believing—stubbornly, impossibly, the way Nova herself believed—that she could protect everyone by absorbing the damage herself.
She stood up.

She found Aria on the catwalk above the central chamber, right where she'd expected to find her—the place where the Heart's glow was most visible, where the green-white light pulsed through the floor grating and turned everything it touched into something that looked almost holy. Aria was leaning against the rail, knuckles pale on the metal, staring down into the light. Her eyes had that slightly distant quality they got when she was monitoring the integration—the fraction of her attention that was always, now, somewhere else, swimming through alien architecture behind her firewall.

She didn't hear Nova approach. Or she did, and didn't turn—waiting, the way Nova had waited in the observation deck, for the person approaching to decide what kind of conversation this was going to be.

Nova stepped up beside her. Didn't lean on the rail. Didn't look at the Heart. Looked at Aria—really looked, for the first time in days, since the confession on the observation deck and the three meters of cold steel and the pulling back. Aria looked thinner. Not physically—she was still eating, still functioning—but something behind her eyes had been rationing itself, conserving resources, pulling everything inward to hold the walls. The dark circles under her eyes. The slight tremor in her fingertips. The way her jaw was set against something constant and invisible.

She was beautiful. She was becoming something else. Both things were true at the same time, and Nova had been refusing to hold both truths simultaneously because holding them meant standing next to someone she might lose and *feeling it*—
But Verdant was right. Feeling it wasn't optional. It was the price. And she didn't get to opt out of the price just because it was expensive.
Nova reached out. Took Aria's hand.

Not the flesh hand—the chrome one. The metal fingers were cool, slightly vibrating with the residual whir of idle servos, and they stiffened under her touch with surprise. Then she took Aria's other hand too—the flesh one, warm and trembling and alive, and held both of them, chrome and skin, metal and blood, in her own grip.

Aria turned. Her eyes were wide. Not startled—shocked, the way you're shocked when something you'd given up on suddenly arrives anyway. The Heart's glow painted her face in green and white, and in that light she looked like two people at once: the woman who'd fixed the scrubbers and recited thermodynamic equations in her sleep, and the thing she was becoming, the architecture opening behind her eyes, the second pulse counting time against her own.
Nova didn't say *I forgive you.* She didn't say *I'm sorry.* She didn't say the three words that were pressing against the inside of her throat like something trying to hatch. She said:
"You're not going in alone."
Aria's breath caught.

"When you integrate—tomorrow, or whenever—when you walk into that thing and let it take you all the way—I'll be right there. Not safe on the catwalk. Not waiting for a report. *There.* And when it's over—" Her voice almost broke. She held it. Fifteen years of holding things together, and this was the hardest thing she'd ever held. "—however it ends. Whether you come back or you don't. Whether you're still you or you're something else. You won't be alone. That's not a choice you get to make for me."
"Nova—"

"You don't get to decide that my presence isn't worth the risk. You don't get to carry this alone because you think that's what love means. You don't get to *choose for me*." She was gripping both of Aria's hands now—chrome and flesh, both of them, her fingers interlaced with Aria's, and she could feel the trembling in the flesh hand and the vibration in the chrome and she didn't let go. "Elena went in alone because she thought she was protecting Verdant, and Verdant spent five years not knowing if she was alive. You hid the integration because you thought you were protecting me, and I spent weeks thinking I was losing you without understanding why. *It's the same thing, Aria.*"

Aria's eyes were bright. Not the integration-bright of the Heart's architecture showing through—human-bright, wet and raw, the light of someone being seen in a place they thought was going to stay dark.
"It's not the same," Aria whispered. "Elena was trapped. I'm *choosing*—"

"That's worse." Nova's voice was iron—the same iron she'd used at sixteen, standing on a crate in the communal hall, telling starving people they weren't going to die. "That's worse, Aria, because you're *choosing* to do the thing that leaves me behind, and you're calling it noble, and it's not noble, it's just lonely. It's just the same self-sacrifice instinct I have, reflected back at me, and I can't talk you out of it because I'd do the same thing, and that's—"

Her voice broke. Finally. After fifteen years of holding it together, it broke on the catwalk above the Heart, with Aria's hands in hers and the pulse of the machine rising through the floor and the light painting them both in green and white.
"That's the part I can't bear," she said. "Not losing you. Watching you choose the same thing I'd choose and knowing I can't stop you because I'd do it too."

The silence stretched. The Heart pulsed. The green-white light swelled and faded in its eternal rhythm, and somewhere in the architecture behind Aria's eyes, a woman who'd been alone for five years was watching, and something vast and patient was aware, and the whole system was turning on its axis like a planet that didn't know or care about the two women standing on the catwalk above it.

Aria didn't kiss her. This wasn't a moment for kissing. This was a moment for something more fundamental—the collision of two people who'd been orbiting each other at a distance, pulling away out of protection, finally allowing gravity to do its work.
She squeezed Nova's hands. Both of them. Chrome and flesh. Held them the way Nova was holding hers, with a grip that said *I'm not letting go even if letting go would be easier.*
"Okay," Aria said. "Okay. Not alone. Together. That's—that's the deal."
"That's the deal," Nova repeated.
They stood there, hands clasped, chrome and flesh and flesh and chrome, and the Heart beat beneath them, and for a moment the pulse of the machine and the pulse of Nova's heart—
—*synched.*

It happened without warning. One moment the Heart's rhythm was its own alien cadence, deep and slow and nothing like a human pulse. The next, something *shifted*—a phase alignment, a resonance, the way two pendulums hung on the same wall eventually find each other's rhythm and swing together. Nova's heartbeat and the Heart's pulse aligned, click into click, and the floor beneath them stopped vibrating and started *beating*, regular and warm and alive, and it was Nova's heartbeat, *hers*, amplified through stone and metal and a machine that could feel it.
Aria gasped.

Not from the integration. Not from the firewall buckling or the system advancing or the architecture pressing against her defenses. From something else entirely—from a sensation that came through their joined hands, traveling up her arms, through her shoulders, into her chest, and landing beside the Heart's pulse with the soft precision of a key finding its lock.
She could feel Nova.

Not integrated—not through the Heart's architecture, not through the neural lattice that showed her processes and parameters and population estimates. Something else. Something that bypassed the system entirely and arrived through a channel the firewall wasn't blocking, because the firewall was designed to keep the Heart *out*, and this was someone coming *in*.

For one impossible second, Aria could feel Nova's pulse from the inside—the rhythm of her heart, the heat of her blood, the micro-tremor in her chrome arm servos and the steadiness of her flesh-hand grip. She could feel the woman behind the leader and the leader behind the woman and the girl who'd stood on a crate at sixteen and promised not to die.
The firewall held. The Heart didn't advance. The integration didn't tick upward—still sixty-two percent, still holding, still *hers*.

But the channel was open. A crack in the wall that wasn't a breach—wasn't the system getting in—was a person getting through. Nova's heartbeat synchronized with the Heart's pulse, and through that sync, Aria could feel her. Present. Alive. Refusing to be on the other side of the door.
"Did you feel that?" Aria breathed.

Nova's brow furrowed. The synch was fading—the Heart's rhythm already drifting back toward its own alien cadence, the resonance dissolving as quickly as it had formed. But the ghost of it lingered, warmth in their joined hands, a memory of alignment that hadn't been there a minute ago.
"Feel what?"

Aria looked at their intertwined fingers. Chrome and flesh. The firewall she'd built to keep the Heart out, and the gap in it she'd never noticed—not a weakness, not a vulnerability, but a door that opened outward. The wall kept the machine at bay. The door let people in.
She'd built a firewall that could do both.
"I'll explain tomorrow," she said. "But I think—I think there might be another way. Something I hadn't considered."
The Heart pulsed. Out of sync now. But not as far out of sync as it had been.

Below them, the machine turned. Above them, the night held. And between them, their hands stayed clasped—chrome and flesh, flesh and chrome—holding on against the dark, holding on against the machine, holding on because holding was the only thing that couldn't be optimized away.
Tomorrow, Aria would integrate.
Tonight, she wasn't alone.
